questions.question: What is REST and what are its core principles?
questions.answer:
REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for building networked services. It's not a protocol — it's a set of constraints that, when applied, make APIs predictable and scalable.

Core constraints:

  • Stateless — each request contains all information needed to process it. Server holds no client session state. This is why we use JWT tokens (client sends token on every request) instead of server-side sessions.
  • Client-Server — UI and data storage are separated. They evolve independently.
  • Cacheable — responses should declare themselves cacheable or not. GET requests should be idempotent and cacheable.
  • Uniform Interface — consistent URL conventions, HTTP verbs, status codes. Resources are identified by URIs. Representations (JSON/XML) are transferred, not the resource itself.
  • Layered System — client doesn't know if it's talking directly to the server or through proxies/load balancers.

In FolioTrack's ASP.NET Core 10 API, every controller action maps to a resource + verb. GET /api/portfolios/{id} returns a portfolio representation. The client never knows whether a cache or the actual controller handled it.

followUps.question: What makes an API RESTful vs REST-like?
followUps.answer: Strictly speaking, a truly RESTful API implements all six REST constraints including HATEOAS — the API response contains links to related actions so clients don't hardcode URLs. In practice, nearly every "REST API" is actually a REST-like HTTP API: it uses HTTP verbs, JSON, and resource-based URLs but skips HATEOAS. Roy Fielding (REST's author) has said most APIs claiming to be REST don't qualify. In interviews, "RESTful" typically just means: stateless, uses correct HTTP verbs, returns appropriate status codes, and has resource-based URLs — the pragmatic industry definition.

followUps.question: What is HATEOAS?
followUps.answer: HATEOAS (Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State) means API responses include links to related actions — clients discover URLs from the response rather than hardcoding them. Example: a GET /orders/123 response includes { "id": 123, "status": "pending", "links": [ { "rel": "cancel", "href": "/orders/123/cancel", "method": "DELETE" } ] }. The client doesn't need to know the cancel URL in advance — the API tells it. HATEOAS allows the server to change URLs without breaking clients. In practice, almost no production APIs implement HATEOAS — it adds complexity and most clients are purpose-built for their API.

followUps.question: REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC?
followUps.answer: REST: standard HTTP verbs + JSON, widely supported, easy to cache, simple mental model. Best for public APIs and CRUD-heavy services. GraphQL: client specifies exactly which fields it needs in one request — eliminates over-fetching and under-fetching. Best when clients have very different data needs (mobile vs web). Adds complexity on the server (resolver functions, N+1 risk). gRPC: uses Protocol Buffers (binary, strongly typed), extremely fast, bidirectional streaming support. Best for internal service-to-service communication in microservices. Not browser-friendly without a proxy. In CS_PM_Service I would use gRPC between internal services for performance and REST for the public API gateway.

questions.tip: Statelessness is the most important constraint — and the most commonly violated. Server-side sessions break horizontal scaling. JWT moves state to the client, which is why it's the standard in .NET APIs.

questions.question: What is the difference between GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE HTTP verbs?
questions.answer:
HTTP verbs define the intended operation on a resource:

  • GET — retrieve a resource. Safe (read-only) + idempotent (same result every time). Response should be cacheable.
  • POST — create a new resource or trigger an action. Not idempotent — calling twice creates two resources.
  • PUT — full replacement of a resource. Idempotent — sending the same PUT twice has the same effect. Client provides the complete new representation.
  • PATCH — partial update. Only the fields provided are changed. More efficient than PUT for large objects. Not inherently idempotent (depends on implementation).
  • DELETE — remove a resource. Idempotent — deleting an already-deleted resource returns 404 but has no further effect.

In FolioTrack's API: POST /api/portfolios creates, GET /api/portfolios/{id} reads, PUT /api/portfolios/{id} fully updates, PATCH /api/portfolios/{id} updates just the name, DELETE /api/portfolios/{id} removes.

followUps.question: What does idempotent mean?
followUps.answer: Idempotent means calling the same operation multiple times produces the same result as calling it once. GET, PUT, and DELETE are idempotent; POST is not. Example: sending PUT /portfolios/1 { "name": "Growth" } five times leaves the portfolio named "Growth" — same as doing it once. Sending POST /portfolios { "name": "Growth" } five times creates five portfolios. Idempotency matters for retry logic — if a network error occurs and you don't know if the request succeeded, you can safely retry a PUT but not a POST without risk of duplicates.

followUps.question: When would you use PATCH over PUT?
followUps.answer: Use PATCH when you want to update only specific fields of a resource, not replace it entirely. If a user object has 20 fields and they only want to change their display name, a PUT would require sending all 20 fields — wasteful and error-prone (client might accidentally nullify fields it didn't include). PATCH sends only the changed fields: PATCH /users/5 { "displayName": "Alice" }. In .NET, use JsonPatchDocument<T> from Microsoft.AspNetCore.JsonPatch for RFC 6902 compliant PATCH operations that support add, remove, replace, move, copy, and test operations.

followUps.question: What is a safe method?
followUps.answer: A safe method is one that doesn't modify server state — it's read-only. GET, HEAD, and OPTIONS are safe. POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE are not safe. Safe methods can be cached freely and retried without concern. The distinction matters for caches, prefetch, and browser behavior: browsers preload links (GET requests) but never speculatively fire POST. In your API, never use GET for operations that have side effects (creating, deleting) — it violates the contract, breaks caching, and can cause accidental data modification from crawlers or prefetch.

questions.tip: PATCH is underused but important for performance — updating a user's display name shouldn't require sending their entire profile. In .NET, use JsonPatchDocument<T> from Microsoft.AspNetCore.JsonPatch for RFC 6902 compliant PATCH.

questions.question: What are common HTTP status codes and what do they mean?
questions.answer:
Status codes tell the client what happened. They're grouped by first digit:

2xx Success:

  • 200 OK — standard success. GET, PUT responses.
  • 201 Created — resource was created. POST responses should return 201 + Location header pointing to the new resource.
  • 204 No Content — success but no body to return. DELETE and sometimes PUT.

3xx Redirection:

  • 301 Moved Permanently — resource moved; update bookmarks.
  • 302 Found — temporary redirect.

4xx Client Error:

  • 400 Bad Request — invalid input (validation failure, malformed JSON).
  • 401 Unauthorized — not authenticated (missing/invalid token).
  • 403 Forbidden — authenticated but not authorized for this resource.
  • 404 Not Found — resource doesn't exist.
  • 409 Conflict — state conflict (e.g., duplicate email).
  • 422 Unprocessable Entity — syntactically valid but semantically wrong.

5xx Server Error:

  • 500 Internal Server Error — unhandled exception.
  • 503 Service Unavailable — overloaded or down for maintenance.

followUps.question: What's the difference between 401 and 403?
followUps.answer: 401 Unauthorized means the request lacks valid authentication — the server doesn't know who you are. The client should authenticate (log in, provide a token) and retry. Despite the name, it's about authentication, not authorization. 403 Forbidden means the server knows who you are (you're authenticated) but you don't have permission for this resource. No amount of re-authenticating will help — you just don't have access. Practical example: calling a protected endpoint with no JWT → 401. Calling an admin endpoint with a valid non-admin JWT → 403. Mixing these up is one of the most common REST API design mistakes.

followUps.question: When do you use 422 vs 400?
followUps.answer: 400 Bad Request is for syntactically malformed requests — the JSON is invalid, a required field is missing, a string was sent where a number was expected. The request itself is broken. 422 Unprocessable Entity is for requests that are syntactically valid but semantically wrong — the JSON parsed fine and all fields are present, but the business logic rejected it (e.g., end date is before start date, account balance would go negative, a referenced entity doesn't exist). In practice, many APIs return 400 for both cases and that's acceptable. ASP.NET Core's [ApiController] returns 400 for model validation failures by default.

questions.tip: 401 vs 403: 401 = "I don't know who you are" (unauthenticated). 403 = "I know who you are but you can't do this" (unauthorized). Mixing these up is the most common REST API mistake.

questions.question: How does routing work in ASP.NET Core Web API?
questions.answer:
ASP.NET Core routing matches incoming URLs to controller actions. Two main approaches:

Attribute routing (preferred for APIs):

[ApiController]
[Route("api/[controller]")]  // api/portfolios
public class PortfoliosController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet]                          // GET api/portfolios
    [HttpGet("{id:int}")]              // GET api/portfolios/42
    [HttpGet("{id}/holdings")]         // GET api/portfolios/42/holdings
    [HttpPost]                         // POST api/portfolios
    [HttpPut("{id}")]                  // PUT api/portfolios/42
    [HttpDelete("{id}")]               // DELETE api/portfolios/42
}

Route constraints ({id:int}, {id:guid}, {id:alpha}) — validate the route parameter type before matching. A request to api/portfolios/abc won't match {id:int} — returns 404 instead of a bad cast exception in the action.

Conventional routing (from app.MapControllerRoute) is typically used for MVC views, not APIs.

projectExample.explanation: FolioTrack used versioned routing via Asp.Versioning NuGet package — the version segment is part of the URL.

followUps.question: What are route constraints?
followUps.answer: Route constraints restrict what values a route parameter will match. They're placed inside curly braces after a colon: {id:int}, {id:guid}, {name:alpha}, {id:min(1)}, {id:range(1,100)}. If the constraint doesn't match, the route doesn't match and the framework tries the next route. This prevents invalid input from even reaching your action — {id:int} means the action only receives valid integers. You can also write custom constraints by implementing IRouteConstraint for domain-specific validation like {countryCode:iso3166}.

followUps.question: How do you handle route conflicts?
followUps.answer: Route conflicts occur when two routes could match the same URL. ASP.NET Core resolves by specificity — more specific routes win over general ones. Literal segments beat parameter segments: /api/portfolios/summary beats /api/portfolios/{id} for the URL /api/portfolios/summary. Constrained parameters beat unconstrained ones: {id:int} beats {id}. If two routes are truly ambiguous (same specificity), ASP.NET Core throws an AmbiguousMatchException at startup — it doesn't silently pick one. The fix is to add constraints or restructure URLs to eliminate the ambiguity.

followUps.question: What is endpoint routing?
followUps.answer: Endpoint routing (introduced in ASP.NET Core 3.0) separates route matching from request dispatch into two distinct middleware stages. app.UseRouting() matches the URL to an endpoint and stores the result in HttpContext. app.UseEndpoints() (or app.MapControllers()) dispatches to the matched endpoint. Middleware between these two calls (like UseAuthorization()) can inspect the matched endpoint to make decisions — this is how endpoint-level authorization policies work. The middleware knows which controller action was matched before the action executes, allowing auth middleware to check [Authorize] attributes on the endpoint.

questions.tip: [ApiController] attribute changes several default behaviors: automatic 400 on model validation failure, infers [FromBody] for complex types, requires attribute routing. Always use it on API controllers.

questions.question: What is model binding in ASP.NET Core and how does it work?
questions.answer:
Model binding automatically maps HTTP request data to action method parameters. ASP.NET Core looks in these locations in order:

  • [FromRoute] — URL route segments (/api/products/{id})
  • [FromQuery] — query string (?page=1&size=20)
  • [FromBody] — request body (typically JSON). Only one [FromBody] parameter per action.
  • [FromHeader] — HTTP header
  • [FromForm] — form data (multipart/form-data for file uploads)
  • [FromServices] — resolved from DI container
// Complex type from body automatically with [ApiController]
public async Task<IActionResult> Create([FromBody] CreatePortfolioRequest request) { }

// Multiple sources in one action
public async Task<IActionResult> Update(
    [FromRoute] int id,
    [FromBody] UpdateRequest request,
    [FromQuery] bool notifyUser = false) { }

[ApiController] inference: With [ApiController], complex types default to [FromBody] and simple types default to [FromQuery] — so you often don't need explicit attributes.

followUps.question: What happens when model binding fails?
followUps.answer: When model binding cannot bind a required parameter (e.g., a route param is missing, body JSON is malformed), it adds an error to ModelState. With [ApiController], ASP.NET Core automatically returns a 400 Bad Request with a ValidationProblemDetails body before the action even executes — you don't need to check ModelState.IsValid manually. Without [ApiController], the action executes with a default/null value and you must check ModelState.IsValid yourself. Binding failure is distinct from validation failure — binding handles converting the raw string to the target type; validation (data annotations, FluentValidation) checks whether the bound value meets business rules.

followUps.question: How do you bind a file upload?
followUps.answer: Use [FromForm] with IFormFile for single files or IFormFileCollection for multiple: public async Task<IActionResult> Upload([FromForm] IFormFile file). The client sends a multipart/form-data request. Access the stream with file.OpenReadStream() — never use file.FileName directly in file paths as it's attacker-controlled (path traversal vulnerability). Always validate file type (check magic bytes, not just extension), size, and generate a server-controlled filename. For large files consider streaming directly to storage (Azure Blob Storage) rather than buffering to memory.

followUps.question: What is custom model binding?
followUps.answer: Custom model binding lets you control how a specific type or parameter is populated from the request. Implement IModelBinder with a BindModelAsync(ModelBindingContext) method, then register it with [ModelBinder(typeof(MyBinder))] on the parameter or type. Common use cases: binding comma-separated query values to List<int>, converting a custom string format to a domain object, or binding from a custom header. A IModelBinderProvider lets you register the binder globally for all parameters of a certain type instead of decorating each one individually.

questions.tip: Custom model binder — useful for binding comma-separated query params to a List, or converting a string to a domain type. Implement IModelBinder and register with [ModelBinder(typeof(MyBinder))].

questions.question: What is model validation in ASP.NET Core and how do you use it?
questions.answer:
Model validation ensures incoming data meets requirements before the action body executes. Three layers:

1. Data Annotations (on the model):

public class CreateInvoiceRequest
{
    [Required]
    [StringLength(200)]
    public string ClientName { get; set; } = string.Empty;

    [Range(0.01, 1_000_000)]
    public decimal Amount { get; set; }

    [EmailAddress]
    public string? ClientEmail { get; set; }

    [RegularExpression(@"^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$")]
    public string DueDate { get; set; } = string.Empty;
}

2. Automatic 400 response with [ApiController]: If ModelState.IsValid is false, ASP.NET Core returns a 400 ProblemDetails response automatically — no if (!ModelState.IsValid) return BadRequest(...) needed.

3. FluentValidation (library) — for complex, cross-field validation logic that data annotations can't express. Supports async validation (e.g., check if email already exists in DB).

followUps.question: What is IValidatableObject?
followUps.answer: IValidatableObject allows a model class to provide its own custom validation logic by implementing Validate(ValidationContext). It runs after all data annotation checks pass and lets you express cross-field rules: "if PaymentMethod is CreditCard, then CardNumber is required". Return ValidationResult.Success or a list of ValidationResult objects with error messages and affected member names. The framework calls this automatically as part of model validation. The downside vs FluentValidation: no async support and harder to test in isolation. Use FluentValidation for anything non-trivial.

followUps.question: FluentValidation vs data annotations — when to use which?
followUps.answer: Use data annotations for simple, format-level rules that the model inherently owns: [Required], [StringLength], [EmailAddress], [Range]. These are declarative, self-documenting, and appear in Swagger. Use FluentValidation for complex business rules: cross-field validation (end date after start date), async validation (is email unique in DB), conditional rules (required only if another field is set), and rules that need DI-injected services. FluentValidation validators are classes you can unit test in isolation — passing a valid/invalid DTO and asserting the error messages. For most production APIs, I use both: annotations for basic format checking, FluentValidation for business rules.

questions.tip: Always use FluentValidation for business rule validation ("email must be unique", "amount must be less than client's credit limit") — these need async DB calls which data annotations can't do. Use data annotations for simple format rules ([Required], [StringLength]).

questions.question: What is CORS and how do you configure it in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a browser security mechanism that blocks a web page from making requests to a different origin (domain/port/protocol) than the one that served the page.

When FolioTrack's Angular frontend on localhost:4200 calls the API on localhost:5001, the browser sends an Origin: http://localhost:4200 header. The API must respond with Access-Control-Allow-Origin: http://localhost:4200 (or *) or the browser blocks the response.

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddCors(options =>
{
    options.AddPolicy("FolioTrackCors", policy =>
    {
        policy.WithOrigins(
                "http://localhost:4200",   // dev
                "https://foliotrack.app")  // prod
            .AllowAnyHeader()
            .AllowAnyMethod()
            .AllowCredentials();  // required for cookies/auth
    });
});

// Middleware — BEFORE routing
app.UseCors("FolioTrackCors");
app.UseAuthentication();
app.UseAuthorization();

Preflight requests: Before non-simple requests (POST with JSON body), the browser sends an HTTP OPTIONS request to check CORS. ASP.NET Core handles this automatically when CORS middleware is configured.

followUps.question: What is a preflight request?
followUps.answer: A preflight request is an automatic HTTP OPTIONS request the browser sends before certain cross-origin requests to ask the server if the actual request is allowed. It's triggered for non-simple requests: when using methods other than GET/POST, when using custom headers like Authorization or Content-Type: application/json, or when the request includes credentials. The browser sends Access-Control-Request-Method and Access-Control-Request-Headers. The server must respond with Access-Control-Allow-Origin, Access-Control-Allow-Methods, and Access-Control-Allow-Headers. Only then does the browser send the actual request. ASP.NET Core's CORS middleware handles preflight responses automatically.

followUps.question: Why is CORS a browser-only restriction?
followUps.answer: CORS is enforced entirely by the browser, not the server. The server returns CORS headers as suggestions; the browser decides whether to expose the response to JavaScript. A curl command, Postman, or a server-to-server request completely ignores CORS headers — they always receive the response. This means CORS is a user protection mechanism (prevents malicious websites from making requests using a user's credentials/cookies to your API) — it's not a security control for API access itself. If you need to restrict which services can call your API, use API keys, mTLS, or IP allowlists — not CORS.

followUps.question: What's the difference between simple and non-simple requests?
followUps.answer: Simple requests skip the preflight and are sent directly. They must meet all: method is GET, POST, or HEAD; headers are only Accept, Accept-Language, Content-Language, or Content-Type (only with values text/plain, multipart/form-data, or application/x-www-form-urlencoded). Non-simple (preflighted) requests are anything outside those rules — like a POST with Content-Type: application/json, any request with an Authorization header, or any PUT/PATCH/DELETE. In practice, nearly all REST API calls from Angular are non-simple (JSON body + Authorization header) and will be preflighted.

questions.tip: CORS is enforced by the browser, not the server. A curl request or Postman call to an API with "wrong" CORS headers will succeed — only the browser enforces it. CORS is not a security mechanism for APIs; it's a browser protection for end users.

questions.question: What are action filters in ASP.NET Core Web API and how do you create a custom one?
questions.answer:
Action filters run before or after an action method executes. They implement IActionFilter (sync) or IAsyncActionFilter (async) and intercept the action pipeline.

Built-in filters: [Authorize], [ValidateAntiForgeryToken], [ResponseCache], [Produces]

Custom action filter example — execution timing:

public class ExecutionTimingFilter : IAsyncActionFilter
{
    private readonly ILogger<ExecutionTimingFilter> _logger;
    public ExecutionTimingFilter(ILogger<ExecutionTimingFilter> logger)
        => _logger = logger;

    public async Task OnActionExecutionAsync(
        ActionExecutingContext context, ActionExecutionDelegate next)
    {
        var sw = Stopwatch.StartNew();
        var result = await next();  // execute the action
        sw.Stop();

        if (sw.ElapsedMilliseconds > 500)
            _logger.LogWarning("{Action} took {ms}ms",
                context.ActionDescriptor.DisplayName, sw.ElapsedMilliseconds);
    }
}

// Register globally in Program.cs
builder.Services.AddControllers(options =>
    options.Filters.Add<ExecutionTimingFilter>());

followUps.question: What is the filter pipeline order?
followUps.answer: Filters execute in a defined order: Authorization → Resource → Action → Exception → Result. On the way in (before action): Authorization filters, Resource filters, Action filters' OnActionExecuting. The action executes. On the way back (after action): Action filters' OnActionExecuted, Result filters' OnResultExecuting, the result executes, Result filters' OnResultExecuted. When registering multiple filters of the same type, global filters run before controller-level, which run before action-level. You can control order with the Order property: lower order runs first on the way in, last on the way out.

followUps.question: Difference between action filter and middleware?
followUps.answer: Middleware operates on the raw HTTP pipeline — it only sees HttpContext and has no knowledge of MVC concepts (no controller name, no model state, no action arguments). It runs for every request including static files and non-MVC endpoints. Action filters operate inside the MVC pipeline and have access to ActionDescriptor (controller/action name), ModelState, ActionArguments (bound parameters), and the controller instance. Use middleware for HTTP-level cross-cutting concerns (logging, CORS, rate limiting, compression). Use action filters when you need MVC-aware logic (log action names, validate model state, add response headers based on action metadata).

followUps.question: What is an exception filter?
followUps.answer: An exception filter implements IExceptionFilter or IAsyncExceptionFilter and runs when an unhandled exception occurs within the MVC action pipeline. It receives an ExceptionContext with the exception and can set context.Result to a response and context.ExceptionHandled = true to swallow the exception. Exception filters only catch exceptions from MVC actions and filters — they don't catch exceptions from middleware or startup code. For broader exception handling, use the IExceptionHandler middleware approach. Exception filters are useful for action-specific error handling where you want different error responses per controller.

questions.tip: Filter vs Middleware: Middleware operates on the raw HTTP pipeline (HttpContext). Filters operate within the MVC pipeline and have access to controller/action context (ActionDescriptor, ModelState). Use filters when you need MVC-aware logic; use middleware for HTTP-level cross-cutting concerns.

questions.question: How do you implement API versioning in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
API versioning lets you evolve your API without breaking existing clients. The Asp.Versioning.Mvc NuGet package (previously Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.Versioning) handles this cleanly.

Three versioning strategies:

  • URL segment: /api/v1/portfolios — most visible, easy to test in browser, my preference.
  • Query string: /api/portfolios?api-version=1.0
  • Header: X-Api-Version: 1.0 — keeps URLs clean but harder to test
// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddApiVersioning(options =>
{
    options.DefaultApiVersion = new ApiVersion(1, 0);
    options.AssumeDefaultVersionWhenUnspecified = true;
    options.ReportApiVersions = true; // returns api-supported-versions header
}).AddMvc();

// Controller
[ApiController]
[ApiVersion("1.0")]
[ApiVersion("2.0")]
[Route("api/v{version:apiVersion}/portfolios")]
public class PortfoliosController : ControllerBase
{
    [HttpGet, MapToApiVersion("1.0")]
    public IActionResult GetV1() { /* old format */ }

    [HttpGet, MapToApiVersion("2.0")]
    public IActionResult GetV2() { /* new format */ }
}

projectExample.explanation: ServiceSelection project used URL segment versioning to add Angular Web Component-specific endpoints in v2 while maintaining the v1 REST API for backward compatibility.

followUps.question: How do you deprecate an API version?
followUps.answer: Mark a version as deprecated using [ApiVersion("1.0", Deprecated = true)] on the controller. The response will include an api-deprecated-versions: 1.0 header (when ReportApiVersions = true) that clients can check. Deprecated versions still work — they're not removed, just flagged as scheduled for removal. Communicate a sunset date (often via a Sunset HTTP response header per RFC 8594) giving clients time to migrate. The standard practice is: deprecate → sunset date announced → version removed after a reasonable migration window (typically 6-12 months for public APIs).

followUps.question: How do you version Swagger/OpenAPI docs?
followUps.answer: Register a separate Swagger document per API version and use the versioning library's API explorer integration. In AddSwaggerGen, add a SwaggerDoc per version: options.SwaggerDoc("v1", new OpenApiInfo { Title = "API v1", Version = "v1" }). Add builder.Services.AddApiVersioning().AddApiExplorer(options => { options.GroupNameFormat = "'v'VVV"; options.SubstituteApiVersionInUrl = true; }). In UseSwaggerUI, add an endpoint per version: options.SwaggerEndpoint("/swagger/v1/swagger.json", "v1"). The Swagger UI will then show a version dropdown.

questions.tip: Always version from day one — retrofitting versioning is painful. Even if v1 and v2 are identical initially, the infrastructure is there. Mark deprecated versions with [Obsolete] and Deprecated = true in ApiVersionAttribute.

questions.question: How do you implement global exception handling in ASP.NET Core Web API?
questions.answer:
Never let unhandled exceptions bubble up to clients as 500 HTML error pages. Two main approaches:

1. Exception handling middleware (preferred for APIs):

// Custom middleware
app.UseExceptionHandler(errorApp =>
{
    errorApp.Run(async context =>
    {
        var feature = context.Features.Get<IExceptionHandlerFeature>()!;
        var ex = feature.Error;

        var statusCode = ex switch
        {
            KeyNotFoundException => 404,
            UnauthorizedAccessException => 403,
            ValidationException => 400,
            _ => 500
        };

        context.Response.StatusCode = statusCode;
        context.Response.ContentType = "application/problem+json";
        await context.Response.WriteAsJsonAsync(new ProblemDetails
        {
            Status = statusCode,
            Title = ex.GetType().Name,
            Detail = ex.Message  // don't expose stack trace in prod!
        });
    });
});

2. IExceptionHandler interface (.NET 8): Cleaner way — implement IExceptionHandler, register with builder.Services.AddExceptionHandler<MyHandler>(), then call app.UseExceptionHandler().

followUps.question: What is ProblemDetails (RFC 7807)?
followUps.answer: ProblemDetails is a standardized JSON error response format defined in RFC 7807. It defines well-known fields: type (URI identifying the problem type), title (human-readable summary, doesn't change per occurrence), status (HTTP status code), detail (specific explanation for this occurrence), instance (URI of the specific occurrence). ASP.NET Core returns ProblemDetails automatically for validation errors when using [ApiController]. Register with builder.Services.AddProblemDetails(). Use ValidationProblemDetails (extends ProblemDetails with an errors dictionary) for model validation failures. Standardizing on ProblemDetails means clients always get a predictable error shape regardless of the error type.

followUps.question: How do you hide internal error details in production?
followUps.answer: For 5xx errors, never return the exception message or stack trace — they reveal internal implementation details to attackers (file paths, library versions, SQL query structure). Return only a generic message: "An unexpected error occurred." Use environment detection: if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment()) { Detail = ex.Message; } else { Detail = null; }. Log the full exception server-side with a correlation ID, and include that ID in the error response so support can trace it: { "correlationId": "abc123", "title": "Internal Server Error" }. For 4xx errors (client's fault), detailed messages are acceptable — the client caused the error and needs to know how to fix it.

questions.tip: Never return raw exception messages or stack traces in production. Log them internally (Serilog), return a generic ProblemDetails to the client. Distinguish between 4xx (client's fault, detailed message is OK) and 5xx (server's fault, generic message only).

questions.question: What is Swagger/OpenAPI and how do you set it up in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
OpenAPI is a specification for describing REST APIs in machine-readable format (JSON/YAML). Swagger is the tooling ecosystem around OpenAPI — Swagger UI renders a browsable, interactive API documentation page.

In ASP.NET Core 8, Swagger support is built-in via Microsoft.AspNetCore.OpenApi. The full UI requires Swashbuckle.AspNetCore or Scalar:

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddEndpointsApiExplorer();
builder.Services.AddSwaggerGen(options =>
{
    options.SwaggerDoc("v1", new OpenApiInfo
    {
        Title = "FolioTrack API", Version = "v1"
    });
    // Add JWT auth button in Swagger UI
    options.AddSecurityDefinition("Bearer", new OpenApiSecurityScheme
    {
        Type = SecuritySchemeType.Http,
        Scheme = "bearer",
        BearerFormat = "JWT"
    });
});

if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())
{
    app.UseSwagger();
    app.UseSwaggerUI();
}

XML comments on controllers/actions appear in Swagger UI if you enable <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile> in csproj.

followUps.question: What is the difference between Swashbuckle and NSwag?
followUps.answer: Swashbuckle generates OpenAPI documents from ASP.NET Core metadata and renders Swagger UI. It's the most widely used option, simple to set up. NSwag does all that plus can generate strongly-typed client code (C#, TypeScript) from the OpenAPI spec. If your frontend team needs a TypeScript client for the API, NSwag can generate it automatically from the spec — no manual DTO duplication. NSwag also has more customization options for the generated spec. In FolioTrack I would use NSwag to auto-generate the Angular TypeScript HTTP client from the ASP.NET Core API — keeping frontend and backend types in sync without manual updates.

followUps.question: How do you add Swagger for versioned APIs?
followUps.answer: Register one Swagger document per API version and use a versioning-aware API explorer. With Asp.Versioning, call builder.Services.AddApiVersioning().AddApiExplorer(o => { o.GroupNameFormat = "'v'VVV"; o.SubstituteApiVersionInUrl = true; }). In AddSwaggerGen, enumerate the versions and add a doc for each: options.SwaggerDoc("v1", new OpenApiInfo { Title = "API", Version = "v1" }). In UseSwaggerUI, add an endpoint per version. The Swagger UI will show a version selector dropdown. Microsoft's Asp.Versioning.ApiExplorer package makes the version discovery automatic so you don't need to hardcode version strings.

questions.tip: Scalar (newer alternative to Swagger UI) is built into .NET 9 and looks much better. If you're on .NET 8, adding Scalar takes 2 lines: builder.Services.AddOpenApi() + app.MapOpenApi(). Worth knowing.

questions.question: What is content negotiation in Web API?
questions.answer:
Content negotiation is the mechanism where the client tells the server what format it wants (via Accept header) and the server selects the best matching format from what it supports.

The client sends: Accept: application/json, application/xml;q=0.8
The server picks the best match and sets Content-Type: application/json in the response.

// ASP.NET Core: add XML formatter alongside JSON
builder.Services.AddControllers()
    .AddXmlSerializerFormatters();

// Restrict what a specific action produces
[Produces("application/json")]
[HttpGet]
public IActionResult Get() { }

[ApiController] default: ASP.NET Core returns JSON by default. If the client requests XML and you haven't added the XML formatter, it still returns JSON (best-effort match). Use [Produces] attribute to hard-code the output format of an action.

followUps.question: What is the Accept header?
followUps.answer: The Accept header is sent by the client to tell the server what media types it can handle in the response. It supports quality values (q-values) to express preference: Accept: application/json;q=1.0, application/xml;q=0.8, */*;q=0.5 means: prefer JSON, accept XML if JSON not available, accept anything as a last resort. The server uses content negotiation to pick the best supported format. The Content-Type header (sent by the server in the response) confirms what format was actually used. For requests with a body, the client sends Content-Type to tell the server the format it's sending.

followUps.question: What happens if the server doesn't support the requested format?
followUps.answer: If the client requests a format the server doesn't support (e.g., Accept: application/xml but no XML formatter is registered), ASP.NET Core returns 406 Not Acceptable by default — but only if you set ReturnHttpNotAcceptable = true in MVC options. Without that setting (the default), ASP.NET Core ignores the Accept header and returns JSON anyway (best-effort). In practice, most APIs only support JSON and either use [Produces("application/json")] to lock it in or rely on the default JSON-only behavior. Returning 406 is more correct per the HTTP spec but requires client-side handling.

questions.tip: Always decorate actions with [Produces] and [Consumes] — this appears in Swagger docs and helps clients know exactly what to send/expect. [ProducesResponseType(StatusCodes.Status200OK, Type = typeof(PortfolioDto))] gives Swagger the response schema.

questions.question: How do you implement rate limiting in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
Rate limiting prevents API abuse by restricting how many requests a client can make in a time window. ASP.NET Core 7+ has built-in rate limiting via Microsoft.AspNetCore.RateLimiting.

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddRateLimiter(options =>
{
    // Fixed window: 100 requests per minute per IP
    options.AddFixedWindowLimiter("fixed", config =>
    {
        config.PermitLimit = 100;
        config.Window = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(1);
        config.QueueProcessingOrder = QueueProcessingOrder.OldestFirst;
        config.QueueLimit = 5;
    });

    // Sliding window: smoother than fixed (no burst at window reset)
    options.AddSlidingWindowLimiter("api", config =>
    {
        config.PermitLimit = 100;
        config.Window = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(1);
        config.SegmentsPerWindow = 4;  // 4x15s segments
    });

    options.RejectionStatusCode = StatusCodes.Status429TooManyRequests;
});

app.UseRateLimiter();

// Apply to controller
[EnableRateLimiting("api")]
[ApiController]
public class PortfoliosController : ControllerBase { }

followUps.question: What is a token bucket algorithm?
followUps.answer: The token bucket algorithm works like a bucket that fills with tokens at a fixed rate (e.g., 10 tokens per second) up to a maximum capacity. Each request consumes one token. If the bucket has tokens, the request proceeds. If empty, it's rejected or queued. This allows bursting — if a client makes no requests for a while, tokens accumulate up to the bucket's capacity, allowing a burst of requests. This is more flexible than fixed window (which resets all at once) or sliding window. ASP.NET Core's built-in AddTokenBucketLimiter supports this: config.TokenLimit = 100; config.ReplenishmentPeriod = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(1); config.TokensPerPeriod = 10;

followUps.question: How do you rate limit per user vs per IP?
followUps.answer: Use a RateLimiterOptions.AddPolicy with a custom key selector. For IP-based limiting: context.Connection.RemoteIpAddress?.ToString(). For user-based limiting: context.User.FindFirst(ClaimTypes.NameIdentifier)?.Value. User-based limiting is fairer for authenticated APIs — a single IP may serve many users (corporate NAT), and you don't want one user's API calls to throttle their entire office. User-based limits also allow different tiers: free-tier users get 100 requests/minute, premium users get 1000. Fall back to IP-based for unauthenticated endpoints where user identity isn't available.

followUps.question: What status code should rate limiting return?
followUps.answer: 429 Too Many Requests is the correct status code for rate limiting. Include a Retry-After header indicating when the client can retry — either as a number of seconds (Retry-After: 60) or as an HTTP date. The client should use this to implement exponential backoff rather than hammering the API. A well-formed 429 response also benefits from a descriptive body: { "type": "https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6585#section-4", "title": "Too Many Requests", "status": 429, "retryAfter": 60 }. ASP.NET Core's built-in rate limiter sets the status code automatically from RejectionStatusCode = 429.

questions.tip: 429 Too Many Requests is the correct status code for rate limiting. Include a Retry-After header telling the client when they can try again. For public APIs, use IP-based limits; for authenticated APIs, use user-based limits to be fair.

questions.question: What is the IActionResult return type vs specific types like Ok(), NotFound()?
questions.answer:
ASP.NET Core gives you several options for returning from an action:

1. IActionResult — most flexible:

public async Task<IActionResult> Get(int id)
{
    var item = await _service.GetAsync(id);
    if (item == null) return NotFound();     // 404
    return Ok(item);                          // 200 + JSON
    // or: return Created(url, item);         // 201
    // or: return NoContent();                // 204
}

2. ActionResult<T> — strongly typed with Swagger benefits:

// Swagger knows return type is Portfolio
public async Task<ActionResult<Portfolio>> Get(int id)
{
    var item = await _service.GetAsync(id);
    return item == null ? NotFound() : item;  // implicit conversion
}

3. Direct type return:

public async Task<Portfolio> Get(int id) { ... }

Returns 200 always — can't return 404, 400 etc. Avoid in APIs.

My preference: ActionResult<T> — gives type safety for Swagger while still allowing different status codes.

followUps.question: What is Results vs IActionResult in minimal APIs?
followUps.answer: In minimal APIs, use the static Results class instead of controller helper methods. Results.Ok(data), Results.NotFound(), Results.Created("/api/items/1", item), Results.BadRequest("message"). For Swagger type inference in minimal APIs, use TypedResults (generic version): TypedResults.Ok(portfolio) returns Ok<Portfolio> and Swagger infers the response type. Combine with .Produces<Portfolio>(200).Produces(404) on the endpoint registration for full Swagger documentation. TypedResults is the minimal API equivalent of ActionResult<T> — it carries type information for documentation tools.

followUps.question: How does [ProducesResponseType] help with Swagger?
followUps.answer: [ProducesResponseType(StatusCodes.Status200OK, Type = typeof(PortfolioDto))] tells Swagger the schema for a specific status code. Without it, Swagger shows an undocumented response. With ActionResult<T>, Swagger infers the 200 schema automatically, but you still need [ProducesResponseType(404)] for the 404 case. Best practice: use ActionResult<T> for the success case and add [ProducesResponseType(StatusCodes.Status400BadRequest, Type = typeof(ValidationProblemDetails))] and [ProducesResponseType(StatusCodes.Status404NotFound, Type = typeof(ProblemDetails))] for error cases. This gives Swagger a complete, accurate API contract.

questions.tip: Use ActionResult<T> over IActionResult whenever you have a specific return type. Swagger will correctly generate the response schema. With plain IActionResult, Swagger can't infer the response type unless you add [ProducesResponseType] attributes.

questions.question: What is minimal API in ASP.NET Core and when would you use it over controllers?
questions.answer:
Minimal APIs (introduced in .NET 6) define HTTP endpoints directly in Program.cs without controllers, using lambda-based handlers:

// Minimal API
app.MapGet("/api/portfolios/{id:int}", async (int id, IPortfolioService svc) =>
{
    var portfolio = await svc.GetAsync(id);
    return portfolio == null ? Results.NotFound() : Results.Ok(portfolio);
});

app.MapPost("/api/portfolios", async (CreatePortfolioRequest req, IPortfolioService svc) =>
{
    var created = await svc.CreateAsync(req);
    return Results.Created($"/api/portfolios/{created.Id}", created);
});

When to use Minimal API:

  • Microservices with few endpoints — low ceremony, fast startup, better performance
  • Azure Functions-style compute services
  • Prototyping / simple webhooks

When to stick with controllers:

  • Complex APIs with many actions — grouping by controller is cleaner
  • Need action filters extensively (model validation, auth, caching)
  • Team familiar with MVC pattern

In the CS_PM_Service microservices project I'd use minimal APIs for the lightweight event processors but keep controllers for the complex management endpoints.

followUps.question: How do you add middleware/filters to minimal APIs?
followUps.answer: Minimal APIs don't support action filters directly, but you can use endpoint filters (similar concept). Implement IEndpointFilter and register with .AddEndpointFilter<MyFilter>() on the route. For global concerns, use middleware as usual. For validation specifically, call app.MapPost(...).AddEndpointFilter<ValidationFilter<CreateRequest>>(). The filter intercepts the endpoint call and can short-circuit with a response. Example: a validation endpoint filter calls FluentValidation and returns 400 if invalid, otherwise calls next(context). Filters chain similarly to middleware and can be applied per-endpoint or per-group using app.MapGroup("/api").

followUps.question: How does model validation work in minimal APIs?
followUps.answer: Minimal APIs don't automatically validate model state like [ApiController] does. You must call validation manually or use an endpoint filter. With FluentValidation, inject IValidator<T> directly into the lambda: app.MapPost("/orders", async (CreateOrderRequest req, IValidator<CreateOrderRequest> validator) => { var result = await validator.ValidateAsync(req); if (!result.IsValid) return Results.ValidationProblem(result.ToDictionary()); ... }). For a cleaner approach, create a generic ValidationFilter<T> endpoint filter that validates any request type and apply it to all routes. In .NET 7+, there's also Results.ValidationProblem() that returns a properly formatted 400 ProblemDetails.

questions.tip: Minimal APIs and controllers can coexist in the same app. Use controllers for your complex CRUD endpoints and minimal APIs for simple webhook receivers or health check endpoints. The performance difference matters only at extreme scale.

questions.question: What is the difference between RESTful and SOAP web services?
questions.answer:
These are two fundamentally different approaches to building web services.

RESTfulSOAP
Architectural style (not a protocol)Protocol with a strict specification
Uses standard HTTP verbs (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE)Uses HTTP POST only (verb is in the XML body)
JSON (most common), XML, plain textXML only (SOAP envelope)
Lightweight, no mandatory envelopeVerbose — every message wrapped in XML envelope
StatelessCan be stateful (WS-* standards)
No built-in error standard (use HTTP status codes)Built-in fault handling (SOAP Fault element)
No formal contract required (OpenAPI optional)WSDL defines a strict formal contract
Browser, mobile, microservicesEnterprise, banking, legacy integrations
<!-- SOAP request envelope -->
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<soap:Envelope xmlns:soap="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/">
  <soap:Header>
    <auth:UserId>12345</auth:UserId>
  </soap:Header>
  <soap:Body>
    <GetPortfolio>
      <portfolioId>1</portfolioId>
    </GetPortfolio>
  </soap:Body>
</soap:Envelope>

<!-- REST equivalent: GET /api/portfolios/1 with Authorization header -->

In modern development REST is dominant — all your projects (FolioTrack, CS_PM_Service, OfflineInvoice APIs) use RESTful JSON APIs. SOAP still exists in banking systems, payment gateways, and enterprise ERPs (SAP, Oracle) — the CSInvoice project called a WCF service which is SOAP-based.

followUps.question: What is gRPC and how does it compare to REST?
followUps.answer: gRPC (Google Remote Procedure Call) is a high-performance RPC framework using Protocol Buffers (binary serialization) over HTTP/2. Compared to REST: faster serialization (binary vs JSON text — 3–10x smaller payloads), HTTP/2 multiplexing (multiple requests over one connection), built-in code generation from .proto schema files, and native streaming support (server-side, client-side, bidirectional). REST is better for: public APIs (browser-accessible, human-readable JSON), simple CRUD, when you need OpenAPI/Swagger docs. gRPC is better for: internal microservice-to-microservice communication where performance matters, streaming data, polyglot environments (auto-generated clients in Go, Python, Java, C# from the same .proto file). ASP.NET Core supports gRPC via the Grpc.AspNetCore package. gRPC cannot be used directly from browser JavaScript without a proxy (grpc-web).

followUps.question: What is WCF and how does it relate to SOAP?
followUps.answer: WCF (Windows Communication Foundation) is Microsoft's framework for building SOAP-based services in .NET Framework. It uses attributes ([ServiceContract], [OperationContract], [DataContract]) to define service interfaces, and generates WSDL automatically. WCF supports multiple transport bindings: HTTP (SOAP), TCP (binary), named pipes (local IPC), MSMQ (queuing). In .NET Core/.NET 5+, WCF server is NOT available (Microsoft didn't port the server side). You can consume legacy WCF services with the System.ServiceModel.Http client package. The CSInvoice project in your work calls a WCF endpoint — you're a consumer, not a host. For new service-to-service communication in .NET 6+, use gRPC (high performance) or REST (simplicity/browser compatibility).

questions.tip: For interviews: Know that REST is the default for modern APIs — lightweight, JSON, HTTP verbs, stateless. SOAP is legacy enterprise territory. The key SOAP concepts to know: WSDL (contract), envelope (message wrapper), fault (error). If asked about SOAP in a .NET context, the answer is WCF (server) or Add Service Reference (client).

questions.question: What is Kestrel and what role does it play in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
Kestrel is the built-in, cross-platform web server included with ASP.NET Core. Unlike .NET Framework which required IIS as its web server, ASP.NET Core applications come with Kestrel baked in — no IIS needed.

Key facts:

  • Built on top of libuv (early versions) and now System.Net.Sockets
  • Implements HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, HTTP/3 (QUIC, .NET 7+)
  • Cross-platform — runs on Windows, Linux, macOS
  • Very high performance — one of the fastest web servers in TechEmpower benchmarks
  • Can run standalone (direct internet) or behind a reverse proxy

Kestrel configuration — set the port:

// appsettings.json
{
  "Kestrel": {
    "Endpoints": {
      "Http": { "Url": "http://0.0.0.0:5202" },
      "Https": { "Url": "https://0.0.0.0:7265" }
    }
  }
}
// Or in Program.cs
builder.WebHost.ConfigureKestrel(options => {
    options.ListenAnyIP(5202);   // HTTP
    options.ListenAnyIP(7265, o => o.UseHttps()); // HTTPS
    options.Limits.MaxRequestBodySize = 50 * 1024 * 1024; // 50 MB
    options.Limits.RequestHeadersTimeout = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(30);
});

InterviewPrepPortal runs at https://localhost:7265 / http://localhost:5202 — both served directly by Kestrel. The CS_PM_Service APIs configure their ports from appsettings.json → HostPort.

followUps.question: When should you put a reverse proxy (IIS/Nginx) in front of Kestrel?
followUps.answer: In production, Kestrel is typically placed behind a reverse proxy (IIS, Nginx, Apache, Azure Application Gateway). The reverse proxy handles: SSL termination (offloads HTTPS to the proxy, Kestrel gets plain HTTP internally), load balancing across multiple Kestrel instances, static file serving, compression, request buffering, and security (hides Kestrel from the internet). It also provides features Kestrel doesn't have natively: URL rewriting, rate limiting at the network level, DDoS protection. Configure the ForwardedHeaders middleware so Kestrel knows the real client IP and scheme from the proxy headers (X-Forwarded-For, X-Forwarded-Proto): app.UseForwardedHeaders(). In development, Kestrel runs standalone — no proxy needed. In containerised deployments (Docker/Kubernetes), an ingress controller (Nginx/Traefik) acts as the reverse proxy.

followUps.question: What is the difference between Kestrel and IIS Express?
followUps.answer: IIS Express is a lightweight version of IIS used in Visual Studio for local development — Windows only, tightly integrated with Visual Studio. Kestrel is ASP.NET Core's built-in cross-platform web server that runs on any OS. When you press F5 in Visual Studio for an ASP.NET Core project, you can choose either IIS Express or Kestrel (the https profile uses Kestrel directly, the IIS Express profile routes through IIS Express as a reverse proxy to Kestrel). For .NET Core CLI (dotnet run), Kestrel always runs directly. There is no "IIS Express" on macOS or Linux. In production on-premises Windows servers, full IIS (not Express) is commonly used as a reverse proxy in front of Kestrel via the ASP.NET Core Module (ANCM).

questions.tip: Key points for interviews: Kestrel = built-in cross-platform web server for ASP.NET Core. In development it runs standalone. In production it runs behind IIS/Nginx. HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, HTTP/3 supported. Configure via appsettings.json → Kestrel.Endpoints or builder.WebHost.ConfigureKestrel().

questions.question: What is ADO.NET? How does it compare to EF Core?
questions.answer:
ADO.NET (ActiveX Data Objects .NET) is the low-level .NET data access framework. It provides direct, raw access to databases via SQL commands. EF Core is built on top of ADO.NET.

Core ADO.NET classes:

  • SqlConnection — represents an open connection to a SQL Server database.
  • SqlCommand — executes SQL statements or stored procedures.
  • SqlDataReader — forward-only, read-only cursor for reading rows one at a time. Fastest for large result sets.
  • SqlDataAdapter + DataSet — disconnected model: fills a DataSet in memory, allows offline manipulation, then updates the DB in batch.
  • SqlParameter — parameterised queries to prevent SQL injection.
using var conn = new SqlConnection(connectionString);
conn.Open();

using var cmd = new SqlCommand(
    "SELECT Id, Name, Salary FROM Employees WHERE DeptId = @DeptId", conn);
cmd.Parameters.AddWithValue("@DeptId", deptId); // parameterised — prevents SQL injection

using var reader = cmd.ExecuteReader();
while (reader.Read())
{
    var id     = reader.GetInt32(0);
    var name   = reader.GetString(1);
    var salary = reader.GetDecimal(2);
    Console.WriteLine($"{id}: {name} - {salary:C}");
}

// ExecuteNonQuery — for INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE (returns rows affected)
var insertCmd = new SqlCommand("INSERT INTO Logs (Message) VALUES (@m)", conn);
insertCmd.Parameters.AddWithValue("@m", "Event logged");
int rowsAffected = insertCmd.ExecuteNonQuery();

// ExecuteScalar — returns single value (first column of first row)
var countCmd = new SqlCommand("SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Employees", conn);
int count = (int)countCmd.ExecuteScalar();

ADO.NET vs EF Core:

  • ADO.NET — maximum performance, full SQL control, verbose code, manual mapping, no change tracking. Use for: stored procedure-heavy apps, bulk operations, when ORM overhead is unacceptable.
  • EF Core — ORM, automatic SQL generation, LINQ queries, change tracking, migrations. Use for: most CRUD apps, where developer productivity > micro-optimisation.
  • Dapper — sits between: uses ADO.NET under the hood, maps results to objects automatically via reflection, but you write raw SQL. Best of both worlds for read-heavy scenarios.

followUps.question: What is the difference between ExecuteReader, ExecuteNonQuery, and ExecuteScalar?
followUps.answer:
• ExecuteReader() — executes a SELECT query and returns a SqlDataReader for iterating over result rows. Use for queries that return multiple rows/columns.
  • ExecuteNonQuery() — executes INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or DDL statements. Returns the number of rows affected. Returns -1 for stored procedures that don't set ROWCOUNT.
  • ExecuteScalar() — executes a query and returns only the first column of the first row as object. Use for aggregate queries: COUNT(*), MAX(), SUM().

Always use parameterised queries (SqlParameter) — never string-concatenate user input into SQL commands.

questions.tip: Always use parameters: Never concatenate user input into SQL strings. Use SqlParameter or Dapper's parameter syntax. SQL injection via string concatenation is one of the most common and dangerous vulnerabilities.

questions.question: What is MediaTypeFormatter (Web API) / IOutputFormatter (ASP.NET Core)? How does content negotiation work?
questions.answer:
In legacy ASP.NET Web API (System.Web.Http), a MediaTypeFormatter is responsible for serialising and deserialising HTTP message bodies — converting between .NET objects and JSON, XML, or other formats.

In modern ASP.NET Core Web API, this responsibility is split into:

  • IOutputFormatter — serialises the response body (object → bytes sent to client).
  • IInputFormatter — deserialises the request body (bytes from client → object).

Content Negotiation:

Content negotiation is the process by which the client and server agree on the response format. The client sends an Accept header indicating what formats it can handle. The server tries to find a matching formatter.

GET /api/products/1 HTTP/1.1
Accept: application/xml, application/json;q=0.9, */*;q=0.8

ASP.NET Core selects the best matching IOutputFormatter and serialises accordingly. If no match, it falls back to the first registered formatter.

Configuring formatters in Program.cs:

builder.Services.AddControllers(options =>
{
    options.RespectBrowserAcceptHeader = true; // honour Accept header
    options.ReturnHttpNotAcceptable = true;    // 406 if no matching formatter
})
.AddXmlSerializerFormatters();  // add XML support
// JSON is registered by default (System.Text.Json)
// Optionally: .AddNewtonsoftJson() for Newtonsoft

Custom output formatter example:

public class CsvOutputFormatter : TextOutputFormatter
{
    public CsvOutputFormatter()
    {
        SupportedMediaTypes.Add(MediaTypeHeaderValue.Parse("text/csv"));
        SupportedEncodings.Add(Encoding.UTF8);
    }
    public override async Task WriteResponseBodyAsync(OutputFormatterWriteContext ctx, ...)
    {
        // write CSV content to context.HttpContext.Response.Body
    }
}

followUps.question: How do you force a specific format in ASP.NET Core Web API?
followUps.answer:
Use the [Produces] attribute to restrict a controller or action to specific media types:

[Produces("application/json")] // always returns JSON
[ApiController]
public class ProductsController : ControllerBase { }

Or use [FormatFilter] + format query parameter to allow format selection via URL: /api/products?format=xml.

questions.tip: Practical: In ASP.NET Core Web API, JSON is the default and most teams never change it. The key concept to understand is that formatters are pluggable — you can add CSV, XML, or custom formats without changing controller code.

questions.question: How do you consume an ASP.NET Core Web API from a console application?
questions.answer:
The recommended way is to use HttpClient via IHttpClientFactory, or use a typed HTTP client. For a simple console app, you can use HttpClient directly.

Simple console app — using HttpClient directly:

using System.Net.Http.Json; // for GetFromJsonAsync, PostAsJsonAsync

using var httpClient = new HttpClient
{
    BaseAddress = new Uri("https://api.example.com/")
};
httpClient.DefaultRequestHeaders.Add("Accept", "application/json");

// GET request — deserialise to a list
var products = await httpClient.GetFromJsonAsync<List<Product>>("api/products");
foreach (var p in products)
    Console.WriteLine($"{p.Id}: {p.Name} - {p.Price:C}");

// POST request
var newProduct = new Product { Name = "Widget", Price = 9.99m };
var response = await httpClient.PostAsJsonAsync("api/products", newProduct);
response.EnsureSuccessStatusCode();
var created = await response.Content.ReadFromJsonAsync<Product>();
Console.WriteLine($"Created with Id: {created.Id}");

// PUT request
newProduct.Price = 12.99m;
await httpClient.PutAsJsonAsync($"api/products/{created.Id}", newProduct);

// DELETE request
await httpClient.DeleteAsync($"api/products/{created.Id}");

With JWT authentication:

// Add Authorization header for protected endpoints
httpClient.DefaultRequestHeaders.Authorization =
    new System.Net.Http.Headers.AuthenticationHeaderValue("Bearer", token);

With IHttpClientFactory in a hosted console app (.NET generic host):

var host = Host.CreateDefaultBuilder(args)
    .ConfigureServices(services =>
    {
        services.AddHttpClient<IProductApiClient, ProductApiClient>(client =>
        {
            client.BaseAddress = new Uri("https://api.example.com/");
        });
    }).Build();

await host.RunAsync();

followUps.question: Why should you use IHttpClientFactory instead of creating new HttpClient()?
followUps.answer:
Two problems with new HttpClient():

  • Socket exhaustion — HttpClient holds a socket open. If you create a new one for every request and dispose it, the underlying TCP socket lingers in TIME_WAIT state. Under high load you run out of sockets. (IHttpClientFactory pools and reuses handler instances.)
  • DNS changes ignored — a long-lived HttpClient caches DNS entries. If the server's IP changes (blue-green deployment, failover), the cached HttpClient won't see it. IHttpClientFactory rotates handlers on a configurable interval (default: 2 minutes).

Always use IHttpClientFactory (or using var client = new HttpClient() only for short-lived console scripts).

questions.tip: GetFromJsonAsync is the easiest way to consume a JSON API in .NET. It's in System.Net.Http.Json (NuGet or included in .NET 5+). No manual deserialization needed.

questions.question: Can you perform INSERT or UPDATE operations using an HTTP GET request? What are the consequences?
questions.answer:
Technically yes — nothing in HTTP prevents you from running DML inside a GET endpoint. But it is fundamentally wrong and violates core REST/HTTP principles.

Why GET should never have side effects (safe + idempotent):

  • Safe — GET requests must not change server state. The HTTP spec (RFC 7231) defines GET as safe, meaning clients, proxies, and browsers can assume it has no side effects.
  • Idempotent — calling GET multiple times must produce the same result (no mutation).

Real-world consequences of DML in GET:

  • Browser prefetching — browsers prefetch links in <a> tags for performance. Every link on the page would trigger your INSERT.
  • Web crawlers / SEO bots — Google, Bing, etc. crawl all GET URLs. They would insert/update/delete your data.
  • Caching proxies — GET responses are cached by CDNs and proxies. Your mutation may be served from cache and never actually hit the server.
  • Link prefetch exploits (CSRF) — an attacker can embed <img src="https://yoursite.com/api/delete?id=1"> in a phishing page. If a logged-in user visits it, the browser GETs the URL and triggers the delete.
  • Accidental double-submits — if a user refreshes, the browser re-sends GET without warning (unlike POST, which shows a confirmation).

The correct approach:

  • Use POST for creating resources.
  • Use PUT / PATCH for updating resources.
  • Use DELETE for deleting resources.
  • Use GET only for reading/fetching data.

followUps.question: What is idempotency and which HTTP methods are idempotent?
followUps.answer:
Idempotent means calling the operation multiple times produces the same result as calling it once.

  • GET — idempotent + safe (read-only).
  • PUT — idempotent (setting a resource to a specific state; calling it 3 times is same as once).
  • DELETE — idempotent (deleting an already-deleted resource returns 404 but has no additional effect).
  • POST — NOT idempotent (each POST creates a new resource; calling it 3 times creates 3 records).
  • PATCH — not necessarily idempotent (depends on whether the patch is absolute or relative, e.g., 'set status=Active' vs 'increment counter by 1').

Idempotency matters for retry logic — if a PUT request times out, it's safe to retry. If a POST request times out, retrying may create duplicates (use idempotency keys to handle this).

questions.tip: Rule: GET = read only. Never mutate state on a GET. If you see code like GetAndDeleteUser(id) mapped to HttpGet, that is a red flag in any code review.

questions.question: How do you secure an ASP.NET Core Web API? What are the key practices?
questions.answer:
Securing a Web API involves multiple layers. Here are the key practices:

1. HTTPS Enforcement

app.UseHttpsRedirection(); // redirects HTTP → HTTPS
// Also add HSTS for browsers
app.UseHsts();

2. JWT Bearer Authentication

builder.Services.AddAuthentication(JwtBearerDefaults.AuthenticationScheme)
    .AddJwtBearer(options =>
    {
        options.TokenValidationParameters = new TokenValidationParameters
        {
            ValidateIssuer = true,
            ValidIssuer = config["Jwt:Issuer"],
            ValidateAudience = true,
            ValidAudience = config["Jwt:Audience"],
            ValidateLifetime = true,        // reject expired tokens
            ValidateIssuerSigningKey = true,
            IssuerSigningKey = new SymmetricSecurityKey(
                Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(config["Jwt:Secret"]))
        };
    });

// All controllers secured by default
[Authorize] // or globally via filters
public class ProductsController : ControllerBase { }

3. Role/Claims-based Authorization

[Authorize(Roles = "Admin")]
[HttpDelete("{id}")]
public IActionResult Delete(int id) { }

// Policy-based
[Authorize(Policy = "CanEditProduct")]
public IActionResult Update(...) { }

4. Input Validation

Use Data Annotations + ModelState.IsValid. Never trust client data. Use parameterised queries to prevent SQL injection.

5. CORS Policy

builder.Services.AddCors(options =>
{
    options.AddPolicy("AllowedOrigins", policy =>
        policy.WithOrigins("https://myfrontend.com")
              .AllowedMethods("GET", "POST")
              .AllowedHeaders("Authorization", "Content-Type"));
});
app.UseCors("AllowedOrigins");

6. Rate Limiting

builder.Services.AddRateLimiter(options =>
    options.AddFixedWindowLimiter("api", opt =>
    {
        opt.PermitLimit = 100;
        opt.Window = TimeSpan.FromMinutes(1);
    }));
app.UseRateLimiter();

7. Sensitive data protection

  • Never log sensitive data (tokens, passwords).
  • Return generic error messages in production (don't leak stack traces).
  • Store secrets in Azure Key Vault / environment variables, not in appsettings.json.

followUps.question: What is the difference between [Authorize] on a controller vs using a global filter?
followUps.answer:
Placing [Authorize] on each controller is explicit but error-prone — a developer can forget to add it to a new controller. Applying it globally via a filter ensures every endpoint is secured by default, and you opt-out specific endpoints with [AllowAnonymous].

// Global — all controllers require auth by default
builder.Services.AddControllers(options =>
    options.Filters.Add(new AuthorizeFilter()));

// Individual controller opts out
[AllowAnonymous]
public class PublicController : ControllerBase { }

Best practice: Default to deny (global [Authorize]) and explicitly allow public endpoints with [AllowAnonymous]. This is the 'secure by default' approach.

questions.tip: Security layers: HTTPS (transport) → Authentication (who are you?) → Authorization (what can you do?) → Input validation (is the input safe?) → Rate limiting (too many requests?). All layers are needed.
